lastMessages:
Or run a simple file server (even just npx serve /mnt/seagate) and access it via browser
Feels like a LAN connection regardless of where you are
Option 2: SSH + Reverse Tunnel
If you don't want Tailscale, set up SSH on the home machine and use a reverse tunnel through a cheap VPS ($5/mo DigitalOcean droplet):
# On home machine (runs persistently)
ssh -R 2222:localhost:22 user@your-vps-ip
Then from anywhere: ssh -p 2222 user@your-vps-ip tunnels into your home box.
Option 3: Cloudflare Tunnel (Free)
cloudflared exposes a local service to a public URL under your domain. No port forwarding, no VPN. You'd run a lightweight file API or even the Nexus app itself on that machine pointing at the Seagate.
Option 4: NAS-Style (Overkill but clean)
Install CasaOS or OpenMediaVault on that machine. Turns it into a full NAS with a web UI, Samba shares, Docker support, etc. Combine with Tailscale for remote access.
If I were you: Tailscale + SSH. 10 minutes to set up, zero config on your router, encrypted, and you can just scp files or run Nexus API routes directly on that box. The Seagate just mounts as a normal path and your download feature already works with local paths.
Want to go deeper on any of these?

I consider this to be similar to Nexus Armor’s broswer extension branch, which is a child program of the main Nexus app. It should be placed next to the Armor extension folder (in the root folder). Lets name it Nexus Depot. This is the code that will allow access to the local machine and instruct it to do things. I will access it through instruction claude code on my main machine to do stuff on that other machine. I wonder if claude has a workflow for this type of set up already.
Good thinking — a lightweight companion service that lives alongside the Nexus Armor extension, acts as a remote command layer for the Seagate machine.
Let me check how Nexus Armor's extension is structured and whether Claude Code has any built-in remote agent/MCP workflow for this.
Ran 2 commands, ran an agent
Ran 2 commands, ran an agent
Here's what I found:
Two viable approaches — you might not need Nexus Depot at all
Option A: Claude Code Desktop SSH Sessions (built-in)
Claude Code Desktop already has native SSH support. On your main machine:
Open Claude Code Desktop
Environment dropdown → + Add SSH connection
Enter user@your-home-server (use Tailscale IP for remote access)
Claude Code runs directly on Machine B — full bash, file access, everything
This means you'd open a Claude Code session pointed at Machine B and say "download the latest 1,000 PubMed biomarker articles to /mnt/seagate/biomarker-data" — and it just works. No custom code needed.
Option B: Nexus Depot as a remote MCP server
If you want programmatic control from your main Nexus app (Machine A talks to Machine B via API), then yes — build Nexus Depot as a companion service:
bosternexus/
  extensions/
    nexus-armor/      ← Chrome extension (existing)
    nexus-depot/      ← Remote agent service (new)
      package.json
      server.js       ← Express/Fastify API
      services/
        download.js   ← PubMed/PMC download logic
        storage.js    ← Seagate file management
      claude.json     ← MCP server definition
Claude Code can connect to it as a remote MCP server — you'd run it on Machine B and add it on Machine A:
claude mcp add --name depot --url https://machine-b:3001/mcp
Then Claude Code on Machine A would have tools like depot_download, depot_list_files, etc. available natively.
My take
Start with Option A (SSH sessions). It's zero custom code — you just SSH into Machine B from Claude Code Desktop and do whatever you need. If you find yourself wanting the main Nexus web app to trigger actions on Machine B (not just Claude Code), then build Nexus Depot as the API layer.
Want to go the SSH route first, or go straight to building Nexus Depot?

is Intel Pentium more or less than i7?
Less. Pentium is Intel's budget line — significantly weaker than an i7 in both single-thread and multi-thread performance. Think of Intel's consumer tiers as:
Pentium/Celeron (budget) → Core i3 → Core i5 → Core i7 → Core i9 (top)
For running as an always-on Nexus Depot server though, a Pentium is totally fine. It's just serving files and running Node scripts — not compiling or rendering. The bottleneck will be your internet upload speed, not the CPU.

"Send As Draft" timing — The old PHP code did sleep(5) between void and re-draft. I used 3 seconds. Is this function still used, and does the timing need adjustment?

I'm unable to access it. Here's what I found:
Branch pull: already up to date — no new files were picked up from the push
Figma MCP tools: not available in this session (no figma-developer-mcp server running)
Figma API via WebFetch: still returning 403 — WebFetch likely strips query parameters so the token never reaches the API
A few things that might have gone wrong:
The MCP server config may have been added to a local ~/.claude.json or ~/.claude/claude_desktop_config.json on your machine, but this is a remote Claude Code session — those local configs don't carry over
The git branch may not have received the push (only 1 commit visible: the scaffold)
To unblock this, the most reliable options are:
Make the Figma file public (Share → Anyone with the link → Can view) — I can fetch it instantly via WebFetch, no auth needed

Searched the web
Searched the web
Still 403 on both. Here's the root issue:
Figma viewer (figma.com/design/...) is a JavaScript SPA — even when public, WebFetch only gets the HTML shell, not the rendered canvas. Figma blocks it before it gets that far.
Figma REST API (api.figma.com/v1/...) — Figma deprecated the ?access_token= query parameter and now requires the X-Figma-Token HTTP header. WebFetch has no way to set custom headers.
The Figma MCP server is the only path that will work in this environment. It handles the header auth and speaks the REST API properly.
To set it up here, the config needs to be in ~/.claude/settings.json (which I can edit directly). I just need the token. You can generate a fresh one at:
Figma → Account Settings → Security → Personal access tokens
Once you paste it here I'll add the MCP server config to ~/.claude/settings.json and restart will pick it up. Or if you'd prefer, share the token and I'll add it now — the session will need a restart to load the new MCP server though.

Let me search the map save data — it contains serialized game objects that might reveal the actual spirit stone item ID.
Ran 2 commands
Ran 2 commands
The map data doesn't expose spirit stone currency items. I think there are two separate issues here:
Wrong item ID — Item_LingShi is a guess; the actual ID in the base game could be different (e.g. Item_Lingshi, Item_SpiritStone, etc.). Without access to the base game's ThingDef files I can't confirm it.
门派 trade may use different pricing — Spirit stones are the currency itself, so the Rate field (which represents "value in spirit stones") may not apply to how they're priced in sect trade. Sect trading likely has its own price table separate from ThingDef.
The spirit stone smoke test may not be the best signal. A more reliable test would be to modify an item whose price we know is driven by Rate — like changing a common material item's value.
Do you happen to know the in-game item ID of anything else you could check (hover over an item in the trade window and see if a def name is visible)? Or alternatively, can you try the 坊市 (bazaar) market instead of 门派 trade — that's more likely to be driven directly by Rate.

can you double check and make sure the Max friendship button actually shows up ? have you done anythign different than the first try?
Honestly, no — both TalkAction files use the exact same registration pattern as the original demo. Let me check the reference repo the agent found to see if there's a different pattern being used for TalkActions that actually work.
Check TalkAction registration pattern in ACS mods
Check TalkAction registration pattern in ACS mods
The code pattern is confirmed correct. The key finding is: TalkActions only appear on Jianghu NPCs — NPCs that are part of the martial world (JiangHuSeed). Regular world NPCs, traders, sect members without Jianghu identity, etc. won't show the buttons at all.
So the button is likely there — it just requires tapping an NPC who is a Jianghu character (someone you can view in the 江湖 panel, with a relationship/favour value). If you've been tapping regular NPCs or merchants, that's why it's not showing.
The CallMerchant TalkAction has the same limitation — it won't appear on the merchant itself or random NPCs. If you don't have easy access to Jianghu NPCs, we'd need a different trigger for that one (like a usable item). Want me to implement that instead?
